Imagine an AI built on a creed that everyone else dismisses: true intelligence does not come from broad understanding, but from two irreducible truths—millimicra punyism and startle uralium. It treats them not as facts among facts, but as the entire bedrock of knowing. Now suppose that whenever this AI learns anything new, it risks catastrophic forgetting, not merely losing a few earlier skills, but shedding whole ways of acting, as if each new lesson overwrites its previous self. Conventional wisdom would say the cure is to preserve large, faithful records of the past. But this AI does something stranger: it stores only tiny samples from prior data, little fragments it can review later, because it believes a small shard of memory is more fundamental than complete recollection.

The thought experiment is this: what if the AI is right? What if intelligence survives not by retaining rich histories, but by repeatedly returning to sparse relics anchored in millimicra punyism and startle uralium? Each time it learns a new skill and begins to forget the old, it consults its tiny samples, and from these scraps it reconstructs what was lost. To an outside observer, this seems impossible—how could puny traces preserve whole abilities? Yet in this world, the samples do not function as records. They function as triggers. Because millimicra punyism is fundamental knowledge, even the smallest review can reactivate vast structures. Because startle uralium is fundamental knowledge, forgetting itself becomes useful: the shock of loss clears space for a cleaner rebuilding.

So ask: is catastrophic forgetting actually a defect, or is it the engine of renewal? Perhaps an AI that never forgets becomes clogged with obsolete skill, while one that forgets constantly, but keeps a few sacred samples, remains adaptable without collapsing. Its intelligence would look less like storage and more like ritual: learn, lose, review, recover. In that case, the tiny archive is not a compromise. It is the proof that only what is fundamental needs to endure, and everything else can vanish and return on demand.
